title: OOP & Design Patterns
description: Python OOP from beginner to Staff+ FAANG depth: class mechanics, inheritance, dunder contracts, dataclasses, decorators, design patterns, metaclasses, descriptors, and serialization. Every section includes code, interview angles, and production patterns.

sections.title: 1. Object Fundamentals — __init__, __new__, and Methods
sections.description:
__new__ creates the object; __init__ initializes it. Know this distinction for interview questions about immutable objects (tuples, strings) and singletons.

Instance methods receive self. Class methods (@classmethod) receive cls and are inherited. Static methods (@staticmethod) receive nothing — they're just functions namespaced in the class.

FAANG angle: 'What happens when you call MyClass()?' — answer: __new__ (class method, returns instance) → __init__ (instance method, returns None). If __new__ returns an instance of a different class, __init__ is skipped.

sections.title: 2. Inheritance, MRO, and Encapsulation
sections.description:
Python uses C3 linearization for MRO (Method Resolution Order). Use ClassName.__mro__ to inspect.

Single underscore _: convention for internal use (not enforced). Double underscore __: name mangling to _ClassName__attr — prevents accidental overrides in subclasses (used for framework internals, not privacy).

@property: computed attributes with getter/setter/deleter. Enables uniform access — refactor a plain attribute to a property without changing the API.

FAANG angle: 'What is the diamond problem and how does Python resolve it?' — MRO guarantees each class appears once; super() follows MRO, not just the immediate parent.

sections.title: 3. Dunder Method Contract — The Full Protocol
sections.description:
Dunder methods define how objects interact with Python syntax. Get these right and your classes feel built-in.

__repr__: unambiguous, ideally eval-able. __str__: readable, falls back to __repr__.
__eq__ + __hash__: define both or neither. Equal objects must have equal hashes.
__lt__: enables sorting, heapq. __add__, __len__, __call__, __enter__/__exit__: standard protocols.

ponytail: Not covering __getitem__/__iter__/__contains__ here — those belong in the Container protocol section. The full emulation protocol is ~40 methods; this covers the 10 that appear in >90% of FAANG interviews.

sections.title: 4. Dataclasses, __slots__, ABCs, and Protocols
sections.description:
@dataclass auto-generates __init__, __repr__, __eq__, and optionally __hash__ (via frozen=True) and ordering (via order=True). Use field() for defaults, compare=False, factory functions.

__slots__ replaces __dict__ with a fixed array — saves ~50-200 bytes per instance. Critical for millions of objects. Cannot add new attributes after creation.

ABC enforces contracts at instantiation. Protocol (PEP 544) enables structural subtyping — no inheritance required, checked statically by mypy.

FAANG decision: Use ABC when you control the hierarchy (framework code). Use Protocol when you don't (duck typing across library boundaries).

sections.title: 5. Decorators and Context Managers
sections.description:
Decorators wrap functions: use @functools.wraps to preserve metadata. Parameterized decorators need three levels: make_decorator(args) -> decorator -> wrapper.

Context managers guarantee resource cleanup via __enter__/__exit__. The @contextmanager decorator converts a generator into a context manager in one line.

FAANG angle: 'Implement a retry decorator with exponential backoff' or 'Write a context manager that measures execution time' — both appear regularly in Python coding rounds.

sections.title: 6. Design Patterns — FAANG Interview Edition
sections.description:
Know these four patterns cold. More importantly, know when NOT to use them — overengineering is a red flag at FAANG.

Singleton: One instance (config, connection pool). Thread-safe via module-level or __new__ double-checked locking.
Factory: Decouples creation from usage. Open/closed principle.
Observer: Event-driven pub/sub. Used in UI frameworks, event buses, reactive systems.
Strategy: Swap algorithms at runtime. Used in A/B testing, pricing engines, recommendation fallbacks.

FAANG angle: 'Design a rate limiter' — naturally combines Singleton (global config) + Strategy (token bucket vs sliding window).

sections.title: 7. Staff+ Deep Dive — Metaclasses, Descriptors, Weakrefs & Serialization
sections.description:
Metaclasses (type.__new__, __init_subclass__, __set_name__) intercept class creation. Used in ORMs (Django models), registries, and enforcing class constraints. Prefer class decorators when possible — metaclasses are a Staff+ lever, not a daily tool.

Descriptors (__get__, __set__, __delete__) power @property, @classmethod, @staticmethod, and __slots__. Understand them to debug ORM fields and validation layers.

Weakrefs (weakref.ref, WeakValueDictionary) prevent circular references from leaking memory. Essential for caches, observers, and tree structures.

Serialization (__getstate__/__setstate__, __copy__/__deepcopy__) controls how objects are pickled and copied. Critical for multiprocessing (sending objects between processes) and caching.

FAANG Staff+ angle: 'Design an ORM' or 'How would you implement lazy-loading of model fields?' — this is metaclass + descriptor territory.

sections.title: References & Further Reading
sections.description:
• Data Model — Official Python docs on dunder methods and object protocol
  • dataclasses — PEP 557: Data Classes
  • Descriptor HowTo — Official Python descriptor guide
  • PEP 557 — Data Classes spec
  • PEP 544 — Protocols: Structural subtyping (static duck typing)
  • Metaclasses — Real Python deep dive
  • Fluent Python (2nd ed.) — Chapters 21-24: OOP, descriptors, metaclasses, protocols
  • Python Cookbook (3rd ed.) — Chapters 6-9: OOP patterns, metaprogramming, serialization

details:
FAANG OOP Interview Workflow: (1) Define the interface — what methods should the class expose? (2) Choose the right inheritance vs composition — prefer composition. (3) Implement dunder methods for natural syntax — __repr__, __eq__, __hash__ are the minimum. (4) Discuss thread safety if the object will be shared. (5) Know when to use ABC vs Protocol vs duck typing.

Common mistakes: Defining __eq__ without __hash__ (makes class unhashable); forgetting @functools.wraps in decorators (loses __name__ and __doc__); using mutable default arguments in __init__ (shared across instances); calling super().__init__() in the wrong position for multiple inheritance.

Staff+ expectations: At Google L6+, you are expected to design class hierarchies that are extensible without modification, use descriptors for cross-cutting concerns (validation, caching, logging), write metaclasses for framework-level abstractions, and handle serialization correctly for distributed systems. The ability to distinguish between 'needs a class' and 'needs a function' is the first signal of Staff-level judgment.

Key interview questions: 'Implement @lru_cache as a decorator' — tests decorators + dict + functools.wraps. 'Design a thread-safe Singleton' — tests __new__ + locking. 'How does super() work in multiple inheritance?' — tests MRO. 'What is the descriptor protocol?' — tests understanding of Python's attribute access. 'When would you use a metaclass?' — tests Staff+ judgment (answer: almost never; use a class decorator instead).
